title: Swiss Federal Criminal Court, Prosecutor v. Ousman Sonko, Trial Judgment, 15.05.2024, SK.2023.23
text:
Context of the case. 

In July 1994, Lieutenant Yahya Jammeh assumed power in The Gambia following a military takeover of the government and subsequently remained in office for more than two decades. During this period, concerns were raised by national and international actors regarding restrictions on political freedoms and the treatment of individuals perceived to be opposed to the authorities.

Reports from various sources indicated that a paramilitary unit commonly referred to as the “Junglers” operated during this time. The unit was alleged to have been involved in a range of serious human rights violations against members of the civilian population, including arbitrary arrest and detention, torture and other forms of ill-treatment, sexual violence, and summary executions. These allegations have been documented in findings and testimonies presented in subsequent accountability and transitional justice processes.

Facts of the case 

The accused, Ousman Sonko, held senior positions within the Gambian security apparatus during this period. He joined the elite State Guard in 1995, providing security for President Jammeh. In May 2003, he was promoted to Deputy Commander of the State Guard and in July 2003, President Jammeh appointed him Commander of the State Guard. In December 2003, the accused became Chief of Communications and was assigned to the Armed Forces Headquarters. In December 2004 he became Commander, with the rank of Major, of the 1st Infantry Battalion. President Jammeh appointed him Chief of Police (IGP) on February 15, 2005. From November 2006 to February 2012 and from May 2012 to September 2016, the accused served as Minister of the Interior of The Gambia. Apart from this brief period in 2012, where he was the accused remained Minister of the Interior until President Jammeh dismissed him without explanation on September 16, 2016. [6.1.1]

The case concerned Sonko’s alleged responsibility for crimes committed against individuals perceived as opponents of President Jammeh’s government. In particular, he is accused of murder under accomplice liability with respect to the 2000 killing of L., with the alternative charge of intentional homicide as a crime against humanity, also as an accomplice [2.1.1, 2.1.2]. He is also charged, with respect to witness G., of multiple counts of rape, grievous bodily harm, coercion, and aggravated deprivation of liberty as domestic crimes [2.2.2] These charges relate to his alleged conduct towards witness G including raping her at her mother’s house multiple times between 2000 and 2002, and at a motel every weekend over a two-month period in 2002, and later interrogating and physically assaulting her over a four-day period in 2005, while he was commander of the 1st Infantry Battalion [2.2.1]. Under alternative charge, his conduct towards G is described as part of the widespread and systematic attack by the Gambian security apparatus against regime-critical segments of the Gambian civilian population, and is charged as violation of sexual self-determination through rape as a crime against humanity, torture as a crime against humanity, and severe deprivation of liberty as a crime against humanity [2.2.3].

He is also charged with domestic crimes for alleged conduct occurring between March 21 and mid-November 2006, in his capacity as Inspector General of Police, under the alternative charges of torture as a crime against humanity as an accomplice to the detriment of B., C., D., E., and F.; for violation of sexual self-determination through rape as a crime against humanity as an accomplice to the detriment of C.; and for deprivation of liberty, as a crime against humanity as an accomplice to the detriment of B., C., D., and E. and F. [2.3.2, 2.3.3]. He is charged with intentional killing, with respect to M. who was killed in 2011 during the accused’s tenure as Interior Minister, as an accomplice to a crime against humanity [2.4.2]. Lastly, he is accused of having co-perpetrated deprivation of liberty and acts of torture of peaceful demonstrators in 2016, when he was Minister of the Interior, qualified as crimes against humanity [2.5]. 

In January 2000, under his command, soldiers shot and killed a man identified in the judgment as L, described as a ringleader of an attempted coup against Jammeh. In the five years that followed, Sonko repeatedly located and raped L’s widow, referred to as G. The assaults included beatings, death threats, two forced abortions, and an incident in which she was held captive for four days.

In 2006, following another alleged coup attempt, Sonko oversaw the unlawfully detention and extrajudicial killing of numerous individuals. In 2016, during anti-Jammeh protests, numerous individuals were arrested. Many detainees were subjected to torture, including the application of electric shocks to the genitals of male detainees.

One detainee, referred to as C, was described as a vocal governmental critic. In 2016, approximately a dozen paramilitary members took her to a prison, where she was held in solitary confinement, tortured, and raped by a member of the Junglers.

After a falling out with President Jammeh, Sonko was dismissed from government. He fled first to Dakar and later to Europe. After an unsuccessful asylum application in Sweden, he applied for asylum in Switzerland. In 2017, Swiss authorities initiated an investigation into his alleged conduct. Six years later, charges were filed. He was charged with multiple counts of crimes against humanity, including murder, imprisonment or other severe deprivation of physical liberty, torture, and rape.

In 2024, the Federal Criminal Court of Switzerland found Sonko guilty of murder multiple counts of intentional homicide as a crime against humanity with respect to L., M., and N,; of multiple counts of deprivation of liberty as a crime against humanity, to the detriment of B., C., D., E. and F; and of multiple counts of torture as a crime against humanity to the detriment of B., C., D., E., F., N., J., O., H., I. and P. [9.3],The Court held that Sonko, as the highest-ranking police officer in Gambia, must have been “aware of the measures taken by the state apparatus against alleged enemies of the state” and had control over the events [8.3.2.1(e)]. Such measures included the arrests and torture of a victim referred to as “C”, a vocal governmental critic, which occurred “in order to prevent her from challenging Jammeh politically” and thus “took place within the context of the systematic attack on the civilian population.” [8.5.3.1.] [8.3.2.1(c)] Though C. was raped during her detention, the Panel denied a cumulative conviction of rape as a crime against humanity, as it found that the rape constituted an isolated act of violence, and the requisite element of a ‘functional connection between the acts’ was not met [8.3.2.2]. It found that the arrest and torture of C took place within the context of a systematic attack against the civilian population [8.3.2.1(a)].

The Court then examined the rapes committed against G after that period, including the four-day detention in 2005. It considered whether these acts were committed as part of a widespread or systematic attack against the civilian population. The Prosecution argued that senior officials committed crimes against women with impunity and that Sonko acted to prevent G from denouncing her husband’s killing and in furtherance of state interests. However, the Court found no evidence the regime “incited hatred against women” or that “rape or sexual violence against women in The Gambia was used by the state or state officials as a means of targeted repression.” [5.4.2.2] With respect to victim G, the Court found no other examples of sexual assault accompanied by imprisonment and torture during the period in question. It thus concluded that “the element of multiple offenses is already lacking.” It held her rapes had no “collective character” within the meaning of a “systematic or widespread” attack, as defined by Art. 264a [5.4.2.4]. Instead, the rapes were “multiple isolated individual acts and not an attack against Gambian women as part of the civilian population” [ Id .]. The Court concluded that the rapes against G lacked the required collective character and constituted isolated individual acts rather than crimes against humanity.

In reaching this conclusion, the Court determined that: (1) there was no evidence showing rape was a deliberate means of oppression in Gambia; (2) the acts of rape did not require the use of considerable resources by the state; and (3) the acts were not necessarily in line with President Jammeh’s political objectives [5.4.2.6]. First, the Court was unpersuaded by media evidence of persistent sexual violence in Gambia during President Jammeh’s rule. While acknowledging that women were “socially oppressed,” the Court opined that “indifference towards women’s rights and equality does not in itself constitute an attack against the civilian population within the meaning of international law” [5.4.2.3]. In addition, the Court dismissed as dispositive that Sonko wore his uniform during the rapes; the attire could be construed merely as a misuse of state resources and a way to intimidate and subdue the victim. Hence, the Court dismissed the charges related to G’s rape [5.4.2.7].

The Court also considered evidence that electric shocks had been applied to the genitals of a male detainee, referred to as E. It characterised this conduct as torture rather than sexual violence. In reaching this conclusion, the Court held “that the electric shocks to the genital area constituted a method of torture and did not qualify as an independent attack on E’s sexual self-determination. Even if [the victim’s] sexual integrity was affected by the electric shocks to his sexual organs, there is no evidence of a sexual motive on the part of the ‘Junglers.’ Sexuality was not the primary focus of this torture; rather, the torturers’ aim was to inflict maximum pain” [8.3.4.1]

Sonko was sentenced to twenty years’ imprisonment, twelve years’ expulsion from Switzerland, and ordered to pay compensation to victims. An appeal has been filed and the appeal trial will open on 30 March 2026 before the Higher Appeals Chamber of the Swiss Federal Criminal Court (FCC).
crime_types: Sexual and gender-based crimes, including conflict-related sexual violence
